metadata.seed: CoVid has created a situation where I have to work from home all the time and I am unsure whether I will be employed next month

patient.query: CoVid has created a situation where I have to work from home all the time and I am unsure whether I will be employed next month

automatic_thoughts: I might lose my job
I can't handle this situation

llm_response.response:
FINAL RESPONSE:
"It sounds like this situation is really weighing on you. Can you tell me more about what you're noticing or thinking right now?"

patient.query: Yeah, it’s just… it feels like I'm constantly second-guessing myself. I used to feel confident in my work, but now it’s like I can’t shake this nagging feeling that I’m not good enough. Every time I get an email or a call, my heart races. It's exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not good enough
I can't shake this nagging feeling

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the nagging feeling is really taking a toll on you, both mentally and physically. When you mentioned your heart racing during emails or calls, that must be incredibly draining. Can you tell me what makes that feeling stronger or weaker?

patient.query: Definitely. It feels stronger when I’m waiting for feedback on something important or when I know there’s a big meeting coming up. I just start imagining all the things that could go wrong, and it spirals from there. But sometimes, when I get positive feedback, it eases up a bit—at least for a moment. It’s just… unpredictable.

automatic_thoughts: imagining all the things that could go wrong
it spirals from there

llm_response.response: It sounds like the anxiety you're experiencing is tied to specific high-stakes situations, and it often feels physical. When you mentioned the anxiety lessens with positive feedback, I'm curious about how that shift in your thoughts might influence the tension you feel.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it does feel different when I get good news. I mean, those moments are nice, but they’re so fleeting. Then I’m back to worrying about the next time. I don’t know, I just wish I could find a way to feel more steady, instead of riding this wave of anxiety all the time. It makes me feel kind of trapped.

automatic_thoughts: moments are fleeting
wish I could feel more steady
riding this wave of anxiety

llm_response.response: It makes sense that good moments can feel so temporary, and that worry about the future can follow so quickly. That feeling of being caught in a cycle can be really draining.

patient.query: Exactly, it’s like I can’t enjoy the good moments because I’m already thinking about what’s next or what could go wrong. I guess I just feel... stuck a lot of the time. It’s hard to break out of that mindset, and it makes me wonder if I’m really cut out for this job.

triggers: thinking about what could go wrong
automatic_thoughts: I can't enjoy the good moments
I feel stuck
I'm not cut out for this job

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're feeling caught in a cycle of anxiety about the future, which makes it hard to fully appreciate positive moments. That sense of being stuck might be weighing on you, and I wonder how often this pattern shows up in your day-to-day life.

patient.query: Yeah, it definitely shows up a lot, especially at work. I find myself replaying conversations, worrying if I said the right thing or if people think I’m not competent. It seeps into my personal life too, like I’m always on edge, waiting for something to go wrong. I really hate that feeling of being trapped in my own head.

automatic_thoughts: worrying if I said the right thing
people think I’m not competent
waiting for something to go wrong

neutral: Ability to resist obsessional thoughts (observable entity)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure at work is really weighing on you, and it's not surprising that it's spilling over into your personal space too. I can see how replaying conversations and worrying about others' opinions could feel overwhelming. Can you tell me more about how those thoughts tend to show up for you?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like a constant loop in my mind. I’ll think about a meeting and then just replay every single thing I said, analyzing if I sounded stupid or if I made a mistake. And then, if I do mess up, I can’t shake that feeling for days. It makes me feel so anxious about the next time, like I'm just waiting for it to happen again. I’m also worried I’ll disappoint everyone around me.

automatic_thoughts: replaying every single thing I said
analyzing if I sounded stupid
if I mess up, I can't shake that feeling
I'm just waiting for it to happen again

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're carrying a heavy weight with you, constantly anticipating what might go wrong or how others might perceive you. That feeling of being trapped in a cycle of replaying and analyzing must be incredibly draining.

patient.query: Yeah, it really is. It’s like I’m living in this constant state of fear, and I can’t seem to break free from it. I want to be more relaxed and just enjoy my work, but instead, I feel like I have to be perfect all the time. It’s so tiring, and honestly, it makes me doubt if I'm really good at what I do.

automatic_thoughts: I have to be perfect all the time
I doubt if I'm really good at what I do

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how overwhelming that feeling of constant pressure can be. It sounds like you're carrying a heavy burden of self-criticism, especially when it comes to your work. Can you tell me about a time when you felt successful despite not being perfect?

patient.query: I mean, there have been times when I’ve gotten good feedback on projects, and I've felt proud in those moments. Like that one time I led a team project, and it actually went well, even with a few hiccups along the way. But right after it, instead of celebrating, I remember thinking about all the things I could have done better. It’s frustrating to feel that way after something that should’ve felt like a win.

automatic_thoughts: thinking about all the things I could have done better
it's frustrating to feel that way

llm_response.response: It sounds like pride was present initially, but then frustration took over. Can you tell me more about what shifted in your mind from feeling proud to feeling frustrated?

patient.query: Yeah, it's like I had this brief moment where I thought, “Okay, I actually did something good!” But then, almost immediately, my brain started picking apart every little detail. I started wondering if people were just being polite or if I lucked out this time. That shift happens so quickly, and it just zaps the joy out of it. It's like I can't let myself enjoy the moment without this heavy cloud of doubt coming in.

automatic_thoughts: I actually did something good
people are just being polite
I lucked out this time

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like there's a quick shift from feeling excited to doubting yourself. Can you tell me if you've noticed this pattern happening in other situations too?
